content:
The set T represents various traversals over binary tree. The set S represents the order of visiting nodes during a traversal.

$$ \begin{array}{ll}\,\,\,\,\, \text {  T } &\,\,\,\,\,\, \text { S } \\ \text { I: } \text { Inorder } & \text { L: left subtree, node, right subtree } \\ \text { II: } \text { Preorder } & \text { M: node, left subtree, right subtree } \\ \text { III: } \text { Postorder } & \text { N: left subtree, right subtree, node } \end{array} $$

Which one of the following is the correct match from $T$ to $S$ ?

options.content: $ I - L, II - N, III - M$

explanation:
For binary tree traversals, the standard orders are:

  • Inorder: left subtree, node, right subtree

  • Preorder: node, left subtree, right subtree

  • Postorder: left subtree, right subtree, node

Now match with the given symbols:

  • $L =$ left subtree, node, right subtree

  • $M =$ node, left subtree, right subtree

  • $N =$ left subtree, right subtree, node

So,

  • $\mathrm{I}$ : Inorder $\to L$

  • $\mathrm{II}$ : Preorder $\to M$

  • $\mathrm{III}$ : Postorder $\to N$

Hence, the correct match is:

$$ \mathrm{I}-L,\ \mathrm{II}-M,\ \mathrm{III}-N $$

So, the correct answer is Option A.

content: Let $n$ be an odd number greater than 100 . Consider a binary minheap with $n$ elements stored in an array $P$ whose index starts from 1. Which of the following indices of $P$ do/does NOT correspond to any leaf node of the minheap?
explanation:
$$ n \text { is odd and greater than } 100 \text { binary minheap array index start from } 1 . $$

$$ \text { Index: } \frac{n+1}{2}=\left\lfloor\frac{n}{2}\right\rfloor+1 \quad: \quad \text { First leaf node } $$

                      $$ \begin{array}{lll} \frac{n-1}{2}=\left\lfloor\frac{n}{2}\right\rfloor & : & \text { Last internal node } \\ \frac{n-3}{2} & : & \text { Internal node }\left[<\frac{n-1}{2}\right] \\ n & : & \text { Last leaf node } \end{array} $$

content: The height of a binary tree is the number of edges in the longest path from the root to a leaf in the tree. The maximum possible height of a full binary tree with 23 nodes is $\_\_\_\_$ . (answer in integer)
explanation:
Full binary tree: Every internal node must have 2 childs.

Maximum height for 23 nodes: Tree must be skewed one end and because of full binary tree every internal nodes must have two childs.

explanation: Number of edges from 19 to root node = 4.

content: Which of the following statement(s) is/are TRUE for any binary search tree (BST) having $n$ distinct integers?

options.content: The maximum length of a path from the root node to any other node is $(n-1)$.
An inorder traversal will always produce a sorted sequence of elements.
Finding an element takes $\mathrm{O}\left(\log _2 n\right)$ time in the worst case.
Every BST is also a Min-Heap.

explanation:
BST can be skewed tree also

In order traversal of BST is sorted sequence. Worst case search cost of BST is $\theta(n)$. Not every BST is min-heap.

content:
The height of any rooted tree is defined as the maximum number of edges in the path from the root node to any leaf node.

Suppose a Min-Heap $T$ stores 32 keys. The height of $T$ is ________ (Answer in integer)
explanation:
Let's break down the reasoning step by step:

A min-heap is implemented as a complete binary tree. This means every level of the tree is fully filled except possibly the last, which is filled from left to right.

The height of a tree is defined as the maximum number of edges from the root node to any leaf node. For a single-node tree (the root only), the height is 0.

In a complete binary tree with height $$h$$, the maximum number of nodes is given by:

$$2^{h+1} - 1$$

For a tree of height 4:

$$\text{Maximum nodes} = 2^{4+1} - 1 = 2^5 - 1 = 32 - 1 = 31$$

Since 31 is less than 32, a tree of height 4 cannot hold 32 keys.

Therefore, the tree must have a height of 5 to accommodate 32 keys.

So, the height of the min-heap that stores 32 keys is: $$\boxed{5}$$.

content: Which one of the following sequences when stored in an array at locations $$A[1],....,A[10]$$ forms a max-heap?
explanation:
A max-heap is a complete binary tree where every node has a value greater than or equal to the values of its children. For an array representation of a max-heap (index starting from 1), the property can be described with the following conditions for each index $$i$$:

If the value of the node at index $$i$$ is $$A[i]$$, then for max-heap:

  • The left child is at index $$2i$$ (if it exists) and $$A[i] \geq A[2i]$$
  • The right child is at index $$2i + 1$$ (if it exists) and $$A[i] \geq A[2i + 1]$$

Let's check each option to see if it forms a max-heap:

Option A: $$23,17,10,6,13,14,1,5,7,12$$

For array $$A = [23,17,10,6,13,14,1,5,7,12]$$:

  • $$A[1] = 23$$: left child is $$A[2] = 17$$ and right child is $$A[3] = 10$$ ✅
  • $$A[2] = 17$$: left child is $$A[4] = 6$$ and right child is $$A[5] = 13$$ ✅
  • $$A[3] = 10$$: left child is $$A[6] = 14$$ and right child is $$A[7] = 1$$ ❌ (10 is not greater than 14)

Option A does not form a max-heap.

Option B: $$23,17,14,7,13,10,1,5,6,12$$

For array $$A = [23,17,14,7,13,10,1,5,6,12]$$:

  • $$A[1] = 23$$: left child is $$A[2] = 17$$ and right child is $$A[3] = 14$$ ✅
  • $$A[2] = 17$$: left child is $$A[4] = 7$$ and right child is $$A[5] = 13$$ ✅
  • $$A[3] = 14$$: left child is $$A[6] = 10$$ and right child is $$A[7] = 1$$ ✅
  • $$A[4] = 7$$: left child is $$A[8] = 5$$ and right child is $$A[9] = 6$$ ✅
  • $$A[5] = 13$$: left child is $$A[10] = 12$$ ✅

Option B forms a max-heap.

Option C: $$23,17,14,6,13,10,1,5,7,15$$

For array $$A = [23,17,14,6,13,10,1,5,7,15]$$:

  • $$A[1] = 23$$: left child is $$A[2] = 17$$ and right child is $$A[3] = 14$$ ✅
  • $$A[2] = 17$$: left child is $$A[4] = 6$$ and right child is $$A[5] = 13$$ ✅
  • $$A[3] = 14$$: left child is $$A[6] = 10$$ and right child is $$A[7] = 1$$ ✅
  • $$A[4] = 6$$: left child is $$A[8] = 5$$ and right child is $$A[9] = 7$$ ❌ (6 is not greater than 7)

Option C does not form a max-heap.

Option D: $$23,14,17,1,10,13,16,12,7,5$$

For array $$A = [23,14,17,1,10,13,16,12,7,5]$$:

  • $$A[1] = 23$$: left child is $$A[2] = 14$$ and right child is $$A[3] = 17$$ ✅
  • $$A[2] = 14$$: left child is $$A[4] = 1$$ and right child is $$A[5] = 10$$ ✅
  • $$A[3] = 17$$: left child is $$A[6] = 13$$ and right child is $$A[7] = 16$$ ❌ (17 is not greater than 16)

Option D does not form a max-heap.

Therefore, Option B is the correct answer.

content: Suppose a binary search tree with 1000 distinct elements is also a complete binary tree. The tree is stored using the array representation of binary heap trees. Assuming that the array indices start with 0, the 3rd largest element of the tree is stored at index ___________.
explanation:
Given a binary search tree with 1000 distinct elements that is also a complete binary tree, it is stored using an array representation similar to that of binary heap trees, with array indices starting from 0.

The largest element in the binary search tree (BST) is located at the rightmost position in the tree. In the context of a complete binary tree with 1000 elements, the rightmost element is at index 510, calculated using $2^9 - 2$, considering that the array index starts at 0.

The second largest element is the parent of the largest element, and the third largest is the left child of the largest. Therefore, the third largest element is stored at index 509, which is $510 - 1$.

Thus, the third largest element in the tree is located at index 509.

content:
Consider a complete binary tree with 7 nodes. Let A denote the set of first 3 elements obtained by performing Breadth-First Search (BFS) starting from the root. Let B denote the set of first 3 elements obtained by performing Depth-First Search (DFS) starting from the root.

The value of |A - B| is _______
explanation:
Given : Binary tree with 7 nodes consider the following complete binary tree

$$ \begin{aligned} & \mathrm{A}=\text { set of first } 3 \text { elements obtained by BFS } \\\\ & \mathrm{A}=\{\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{~b}, \mathrm{c}\} \\\\ & \mathrm{B}=\text { set of first } 3 \text { elements obtained by DFS } \\\\ & \mathrm{B}=\{a, b, d\} \\\\ & \mathrm{A}-\mathrm{B}=\{\mathrm{c}\} \\\\ & |\mathrm{A}-\mathrm{B}|=1 \end{aligned} $$

Hence, the correct answer is 1.

content:
Consider the following statements.

S1 : The sequence of procedure calls corresponds to a preorder traversal of the activation tree.

S2 : The sequence of procedure returns corresponds to a postorder traversal of the activation tree.

Which one of the following options is correct?

options.content: S<sub>1</sub> is true and S<sub>2</sub> is true
S<sub>1</sub> is false and S<sub>2</sub> is true
S<sub>1</sub> is false and S<sub>2</sub> is false
S<sub>1</sub> is true and S<sub>2</sub> is false

explanation:
To determine the correctness of statements S₁ and S₂, let's analyze each one in the context of activation trees and traversal orders.

Understanding Activation Trees

An activation tree represents the execution of procedures (or functions) in a program. Each node in the tree corresponds to a specific activation (or call) of a procedure, and the edges represent the caller-callee relationships. The root of the tree is typically the initial procedure (like the main function), and the tree reflects the dynamic call structure during program execution.

Statement S₁:

S₁: The sequence of procedure calls corresponds to a preorder traversal of the activation tree.

Analysis:

Preorder Traversal: In preorder traversal, we visit a node before its children. The order is: Node → Left Subtree → Right Subtree.

Procedure Calls: When a procedure is called, it may call other procedures before completing. The sequence of calls unfolds as follows:

A procedure is called (visit the node).

It may call another procedure (traverse to the child).

This process continues recursively.

Correspondence: The sequence of procedure calls naturally aligns with a preorder traversal because:

We "visit" (call) a procedure before calling its sub-procedures.

The call stack grows as we go deeper into nested calls, mirroring the preorder traversal order.

Conclusion: S₁ is true.

Statement S₂:

S₂: The sequence of procedure returns corresponds to a postorder traversal of the activation tree.

Analysis:

Postorder Traversal: In postorder traversal, we visit the children before the node itself. The order is: Left Subtree → Right Subtree → Node.

Procedure Returns: A procedure cannot return until all the procedures it called have returned. The sequence of returns unfolds as follows:

The deepest nested procedure completes execution and returns (visit the deepest child first).

Control returns to the caller procedure, which may then complete and return.

This process continues up the call stack.

Correspondence: The sequence of procedure returns aligns with a postorder traversal because:

We complete (return from) all sub-procedures before completing the current procedure.

The call stack unwinds in the reverse order of calls, visiting children before the parent, consistent with postorder traversal.

Conclusion: S₂ is true.

Final Answer:

Both statements S₁ and S₂ are true. Therefore, the correct option is:

Option A: S₁ is true and S₂ is true

content: What is the worst case time complexity of inserting n<sup>2</sup> elements into an AVL-tree with n elements initially?
explanation:
To determine the worst-case time complexity of inserting $ n^2 $ elements into an AVL tree that initially contains $ n $ elements, we'll analyze the time it takes to insert each element and sum these times.

Understanding AVL Trees:

AVL Tree Properties:

Height-Balanced Binary Search Tree: Ensures that the heights of the two child subtrees of any node differ by at most one.

Operations Time Complexity:

Insertion: $ O(\log k) $, where $ k $ is the number of nodes in the tree at the time of insertion.

Balancing: After insertion, rebalancing the tree requires at most $ O(1) $ rotations, which is $ O(1) $ time.

Analyzing the Insertion of $ n^2 $ Elements:

Initial Tree Size: $ n $

Total Number of Insertions: $ n^2 $

Tree Size After Insertions: $ n + n^2 = n^2 + n $

Time to Insert Each Element:

The time to insert the $ i^{th} $ element is $ O(\log(N_i)) $, where $ N_i $ is the size of the tree before the $ i^{th} $ insertion.

Since the tree grows by one with each insertion, $ N_i = n + i - 1 $.

Total Time Complexity ($ T $):

$ T = \sum_{i=1}^{n^2} O\left( \log (n + i - 1) \right) $

Calculating the Total Time Complexity:

Step 1: Estimate the Range of $ \log (n + i - 1) $

Minimum Value:

When $ i = 1 $:

$ \log (n + 1 - 1) = \log n $

Maximum Value:

When $ i = n^2 $:

$ \log (n + n^2 - 1) \approx \log n^2 = 2 \log n $

Step 2: Approximate the Sum

The logarithm function increases slowly, so over the range from $ n $ to $ n^2 + n - 1 $, $ \log (n + i - 1) $ is between $ \log n $ and $ 2 \log n $.

Average Value of $ \log (n + i - 1) $:

Approximately $ \frac{1}{2} ( \log n + 2 \log n ) = \frac{3}{2} \log n $

Step 3: Compute the Total Time

Total Time:

$ T = n^2 \times O(\log n) = O(n^2 \log n) $

Conclusion:

The total worst-case time complexity for inserting $ n^2 $ elements into an AVL tree that initially contains $ n $ elements is $ \Theta(n^2 \log n) $.

This is because each insertion takes $ O(\log k) $ time, where $ k $ is the size of the tree at that point, and we sum this over all $ n^2 $ insertions.

Answer: Option B

    $\Theta\left(n^2 \log n\right)$

If the no of leaves in a tree is not a power of 2,then the tree is not a binary tree.

Consider a binary search tree (BST) with $n$ leaf nodes ( $n>0$ ). Given any node $V$, the key present in the node is denoted as $\operatorname{Val}(V)$. All the keys present in the given BST are distinct. The keys belong to the set of real numbers.

For a node $V$, let $\operatorname{Suc}(V)$ denote the node that is its inorder successor. If a node $V$ does not have an inorder successor, then $\operatorname{Suc}(V)$ is NULL. As there are no duplicates, if $\operatorname{Suc}(V)$ is not NULL, then $\operatorname{Val}(V)<\operatorname{Val}(\operatorname{Suc}(V))$.

Corresponding to every leaf node $L_i$ that has a non-NULL $\operatorname{Suc}\left(L_i\right)$, a new key $k_i$ with the following property is to be inserted into the BST.

$$ \operatorname{Val}\left(L_i\right) < k_i < \operatorname{Val}\left(\operatorname{Suc}\left(L_i\right)\right) $$

Let $K$ represent the list of all such new keys to be inserted into the BST.

Which of the following statements is/are true?

options.content: $K$ will have at least one element
After inserting all keys from $K$, the height of the BST can increase at most by one
Number of nodes in the BST will double after inserting all keys from $K$

explanation:
Check duplicates in $K$

  • Each $k_i$ is strictly between a leaf node $L_i$ and its inorder successor $\operatorname{Suc}\left(L_i\right)$.

  • Since all keys in the BST are distinct and the real numbers are dense, you can always pick a unique $k_i$ in each interval.

  • Therefore, $K$ cannot have duplicates.

Statement (a) is true.

Will $K$ have at least one element?

  • Only leaf nodes $L_i$ with a non-NULL successor generate a new key.

  • If the BST has only one node, or the largest leaf has no successor, it may happen that no leaf has a successor.

  • So $K$ may be empty in some cases.

Statement (b) is not necessarily true.

Effect on height of BST

  • Inserting a key $k_i$ between Li and $\operatorname{Suc}\left(L_i\right)$ means $k_i$ will always be a child of some leaf $L_i$ (or somewhere along the path to the successor).

  • Since each new key is inserted as a new leaf, the height can increase by at most 1.

  • Example: if $L_i$ is at the maximum depth, adding $k_i$ as its child increases height by 1 .

  • Other insertions do not increase the height further.

Statement (c) is true.

Number of nodes doubling

  • Let's denote:

  • Number of original leaf nodes $=n$

  • Number of new keys inserted = number of leaves with non-NULL successor = $n$

  • The total number of nodes after insertion $=$ original nodes +n (may be less if some leaves have no successor).

  • So the number of nodes does not necessarily double. Statement (d) is false.

content: Let $P$ be the set of all integers from 1 to 15 . Consider any order of insertion of the elements of $P$ into a binary search tree that creates a complete binary tree. Which one of the following elements can NEVER be the third element that is inserted?
explanation:
Complete binary tree with keys 1 to 15 is binary search tree

  • First key to insert is 8 .

  • $2^{\text {nd }}$ keys must be 4 or 12 .

  • If $2^{\text {nd }}$ key 4 then 3rd key can be any of $2,6,12$.

  • If $2^{\text {nd }}$ key 12 then 3rd key can be any of $4,10,14$.

$$ 50,25,13,40,30,47,75,60,70,80,77 $$

Note: The position begins with 1.
explanation:
$$ \text { Preorder: } 50,25,13,40,30,47,75,60,70,80,77 $$

$$ \text { Postorder ⇒ Left, right, node } $$

$$ \text { The position of element } 60 \text { in the postorder }=7 \text {. } $$

content:
A meld operation on two instances of a data structure combines them into one single instance of the same data structure. Consider the following data structures:

P: Unsorted doubly linked list with pointers to the head node and tail node of the list.

Q: Min-heap implemented using an array.

R: Binary Search Tree.

Which ONE of the following options gives the worst-case time complexities for meld operation on instances of size $n$ of these data structures?
explanation:
Let’s analyze each data structure and the natural way to "meld" (combine) two instances of size $$n$$.

(P) Unsorted Doubly Linked List

Because we have pointers to both the head and tail, we can merge two lists by simply linking the tail of one to the head of the other.

This pointer adjustment takes only a constant number of steps, so the meld runs in

$$\Theta(1).$$

(Q) Min‑heap Implemented Using an Array

A straightforward approach is to concatenate the two arrays (each of size $$n$$) to form an array of size $$2n$$.

Then we can use the bottom‑up “build‑heap” algorithm, which runs in linear time with respect to the number of elements.

Hence, the melding takes

$$\Theta(n).$$

(R) Binary Search Tree

One optimal method is:

Perform an in‑order traversal of each BST (costing $$\Theta(n)$$ each) to obtain two sorted lists.

Merge the two sorted lists in $$\Theta(n)$$ time.

Use the sorted array to build a balanced BST in $$\Theta(n)$$ time.

Overall, this approach takes

$$\Theta(n).$$

Putting it together, the worst‑case time complexities for meld on instances of size $$n$$ are:

  P: $$\Theta(1)$$, Q: $$\Theta(n)$$, R: $$\Theta(n)$$.

Thus, the correct answer is Option A.

content:
You are given a set $V$ of distinct integers. A binary search tree $T$ is created by inserting all elements of $V$ one by one, starting with an empty tree. The tree $T$ follows the convention that, at each node, all values stored in the left subtree of the node are smaller than the value stored at the node. You are not aware of the sequence in which these values were inserted into $T$, and you do not have access to $T$.

Which one of the following statements is TRUE?

options.content: Inorder traversal of $T$ can be determined from $V$
Root node of $T$ can be determined from $V$
Preorder traversal of $T$ can be determined from $V$
Postorder traversal of $T$ can be determined from $V$

explanation:
Given a set of distinct integers $V$ and a binary search tree $T$ formed by inserting the elements one by one, let's evaluate which statement about the traversals or the root node of $T$ is true:

Firstly, let’s review the properties and characteristics of the binary search tree and its traversals:

1. Inorder Traversal: In a binary search tree, performing an inorder traversal (left, root, right) will result in a sorted sequence of the elements. Therefore, no matter the insertion sequence, the inorder traversal will always be the sorted version of $V$. Hence, if we know set $V$, we can determine the inorder traversal of $T$ by simply sorting $V$.

2. Root Node: The root node of the tree $T$ primarily depends on the first element inserted into the tree. Since we do not know the sequence of insertions, we cannot determine the root node just from knowing the set $V$.

3. Preorder Traversal: The preorder traversal (root, left, right) depends on the order in which elements are inserted into the tree. Since we do not know the insertion sequence, we cannot determine the preorder traversal from $V$ alone.

4. Postorder Traversal: Similarly, the postorder traversal (left, right, root) also depends on the sequence of insertions. Without knowing that sequence, we cannot determine the postorder traversal from $V$ alone.

Given these points, the only statement that is definitively true is:

Option A: Inorder traversal of $T$ can be determined from $V$.

To summarize, the inorder traversal of a binary search tree for a set of distinct integers will always be the sorted version of that set.

content: Consider a binary min-heap containing 105 distinct elements. Let k be the index (in the underlying array) of the maximum element stored in the heap. The number of possible values of k is
explanation:
In a binary min-heap, the smallest element is at the root, and each parent node is smaller than its children. This property ensures that the maximum element will always be located at one of the leaf nodes because if it were an internal node, at least one of its children would have to be larger than it, contradicting the min-heap property.

For a binary heap stored in a 1-indexed array, the indices of the leaf nodes can be determined by considering the height of the heap. For a complete binary tree with n nodes, leaf nodes start from index $$\left\lceil \frac{n}{2} \right\rceil$$ to $$n$$.

Given that we have 105 distinct elements, the leaf nodes start from: $$\left\lceil \frac{105}{2} \right\rceil = 53$$. Therefore, the leaf nodes are located from index 53 to 105.

The number of leaf nodes (and hence the possible positions of the maximum element) is therefore: $$105 - 53 + 1 = 53$$.

So, the number of possible values of k is 53, making the correct answer:

Option A

content:
Let A be a priority queue for maintaining a set of elements. Suppose A is implemented using a max-heap data structure. The operation EXTRACT-MAX(A) extracts and deletes the maximum element from A. The operation INSERT(A, key) inserts a new element key in A. The properties of a max-heap are preserved at the end of each of these operations.

When A contains n elements, which one of the following statements about the worst case running time of these two operations is TRUE?

options.content: Both EXTRACT-MAX(A) and INSERT(A, key) run in $$O(1)$$.
Both EXTRACT-MAX(A) and INSERT(A, key) run in $$O(\log(n))$$.
EXTRACT-MAX(A) runs in $$O(1)$$ whereas INSERT(A, key) runs in $$O(n)$$.
EXTRACT-MAX(A) runs in $$O(1)$$ whereas INSERT(A, key) runs in $$O(\log(n))$$.

explanation:
To determine the correct answer regarding the worst-case running times for the operations EXTRACT-MAX(A) and INSERT(A, key) on a max-heap, let's analyze each operation in detail.

EXTRACT-MAX(A):

The EXTRACT-MAX(A) operation involves the following steps:

  • Remove the root element of the max-heap, which is the maximum element. This step itself takes $$O(1)$$ time.
  • Move the last element of the heap to the root position.
  • Restore the max-heap property by "heapifying" the root element, which ensures the new root is correctly placed. Heapifying an element takes $$O(\log(n))$$ time because it may need to be compared and swapped down through the height of the heap (which is $$\log(n)$$ for a complete binary tree).

Therefore, the worst-case running time for EXTRACT-MAX(A) is $$O(\log(n))$$.

INSERT(A, key):

The INSERT(A, key) operation involves the following steps:

  • Add the new element at the end of the heap.
  • Restore the max-heap property by "bubbling up" the new element, which ensures it is correctly placed. Bubbling up could potentially involve comparing and swapping elements up through the height of the heap. This process also takes $$O(\log(n))$$ time in the worst case.

Therefore, the worst-case running time for INSERT(A, key) is $$O(\log(n))$$.

Conclusion:

The correct choice is:

Option B

Both EXTRACT-MAX(A) and INSERT(A, key) run in $$O(\log(n))$$.

content:
Consider the C function foo and the binary tree shown.

typedef struct node {
    int val;
    struct node *left, *right;
} node;
int foo(node *p) {
    int retval;
    if (p == NULL)
        return 0;
    else {
        retval = p->val + foo(p->left) + foo(p->right);
        printf("%d ", retval);
        return retval;
    }
}

When foo is called with a pointer to the root node of the given binary tree, what will it print?
explanation:
Given : Retval $=P \rightarrow$ val $+foo(P \rightarrow$ left $)+foo(P \rightarrow$ right $)$

Now, considering given tree with root 10 .

$$ \text { Retval }=10+foo(P \rightarrow \text { left })+foo(P \rightarrow \text { right }) $$

So, until we execute leaf node we won't get to return to root node.

Also child-Nodes 3,8 and 13 will return value of leaf nodes, i.e. 3,8 and 13 only.

So, 5 node will return $5+3+8=16$.

11 node will return $11+13=24$

10 node will return $10+16+24=50$

Since there's no rule about evaluation order of parameters of ' + ' but considering/assuming left right rule by default we get output as :

$3,8,16,13,24,50$.

Hence, the correct option is $(\mathrm{C})$.

content: Consider the array representation of a binary min-heap containing 1023 elements. The minimum number of comparisons required to find the maximum in the heap is _______.
explanation:
Given : A binary min-heap containing 1023 elements.

Key Point :

Min-heap means, parents should be minimum or equal to its children so, max children could be either left or right one.

Following this logic maximum can be definitely at leaf node.

No. of element in leaf

$$ \left\lceil\frac{n}{2}\right\rceil=\left\lceil\frac{1023}{2}\right\rceil=511.5 \approx 512 $$

To find maximum among 512 elements, no. of comparison needed is 511 .

Hence, the correct answer is 511 .

content: In a balanced binary search tree with n elements, what is the worst case time complexity of reporting all elements in range [a, b]? Assume that the number of reported elements is k.
explanation:
Understanding the Problem

Balanced BST: A binary search tree where the height is $ O(\log n) $.

Objective: Report all elements between $ a $ and $ b $.

Constraints:

The number of elements to report is $ k $.

We need to find the time complexity in terms of $ n $ and $ k $.

Algorithm to Report Elements in Range $[a, b]$

Search for the Starting Point:

Find the node with the smallest key greater than or equal to $ a $.

This can be done using a standard BST search algorithm.

Time Complexity: $ O(\log n) $ (since the tree is balanced).

Traverse and Report Elements:

Perform an in-order traversal starting from the node found in step 1.

Continue the traversal and report nodes until a node with a key greater than $ b $ is encountered.

Since an in-order traversal of a BST visits nodes in sorted order, this ensures all keys in $[a, b]$ are reported.

Time Complexity:

Each node visited during traversal contributes to the time complexity.

We visit and process only the nodes within the range $[a, b]$.

Total Nodes Visited: $ k $ (the number of elements to report).

Combining the Steps:

Total Time Complexity: $ O(\log n) + O(k) $.

Worst-Case Analysis

Initial Search:

Even in the worst case, the time to find the starting node is $ O(\log n) $.

Traversal:

In the worst case, we may need to report all $ n $ elements (if $ k = n $).

However, since we only traverse nodes within the range, the traversal time is $ O(k) $.

Total Worst-Case Time Complexity:

$ O(\log n + k) $.

Answering the Options

Option A: $ \Theta(\log n) $

Incorrect: Ignores the time to report $ k $ elements.

Option B: $ \Theta(\log n + k) $

Correct: Accounts for both the initial search and the reporting time.

Option C: $ \Theta(k \log n) $

Incorrect: Implies we perform $ \log n $ time for each of the $ k $ elements, which is not necessary.

Option D: $ \Theta(n \log k) $

Incorrect: Suggests the time depends on $ n $ multiplied by $ \log k $, which overestimates the complexity.

Final Answer

Option B: $ \Theta(\log n + k) $

content: Let T be a full binary tree with 8 leaves. (A full binary tree has every level full). Suppose two leaves a and b of T are chosen uniformly and independently at random. The expected value of the distance between a and b in T (i.e., the number of edges in the unique path between a and b) is (rounded off to 2 decimal places) _____.
explanation:
To find the expected distance between two randomly selected leaves $ a $ and $ b $ in a full binary tree $ T $ with 8 leaves, we need to calculate the average distance over all possible pairs.

Understanding the Tree Structure:

Height of the tree: Since there are 8 leaves and the tree is perfect, the height $ h $ is 3 (levels are numbered from 0 at the root).

Levels:

Level 0: Root

Level 1: 2 nodes

Level 2: 4 nodes

Level 3: 8 leaves (numbered from 0 to 7)

Calculating Distances Between Leaves:

Distance Formula:

$ \text{Distance}(a, b) = 2 \times (\text{Depth}(a) + \text{Depth}(b) - 2 \times \text{Depth}(\text{LCA}(a, b))) $

Since all leaves are at depth 3:

$ \text{Distance}(a, b) = 6 - 2 \times \text{Depth}(\text{LCA}(a, b)) $

Possible Distances:

Distance 0: When $ a = b $

Distance 2: When $ a $ and $ b $ are siblings (LCA at depth 2)

Distance 4: When $ a $ and $ b $ are cousins (LCA at depth 1)

Distance 6: When $ a $ and $ b $ are in different subtrees of the root (LCA at depth 0)

Counting Ordered Pairs for Each Distance:

Total Ordered Pairs: $ 8 \times 8 = 64 $

Pairs where $ a = b $: 8 pairs (Distance 0)

Distance 2: 8 pairs

Distance 4: 16 pairs

Distance 6: 32 pairs

Calculating Expected Distance:

$ \begin{align*} \text{E}[D] &= \frac{1}{64} \left( (8 \times 0) + (8 \times 2) + (16 \times 4) + (32 \times 6) \right) \\ &= \frac{1}{64} (0 + 16 + 64 + 192) \\ &= \frac{272}{64} \\ &= 4.25 \end{align*} $

Rounded off to 2 decimal places, the expected distance is $ \boxed{4.25} $.
